Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered. I'd try to set aside time on weekends since I work during the week, but it rarely went as planned. I'm a visual learner, so I'd spend a lot of time making diagrams and charts from the material, which took longer than just reading. With 12 courses going on, I basically just juggled whatever was most urgent at any given moment. I aimed for about 12 hours a week, but some weeks it was way less depending on what else was happening in my life.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines. I'd love to say I was consistent, but the truth is I'm 25 with a lot going on outside of school, and money's tight so sometimes work had to come first. When multiple assignments lined up, I'd cram everything into a few intense days. Other weeks when nothing was due, I'd barely touch the coursework. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I survived.
Q3: Usually a deadline or a notification about an upcoming assignment would prompt me to log in. Sometimes I'd get a message from a classmate in our peer support group asking about something, and that would remind me to check in. I wasn't someone who logged in just to browse around—it was always purpose-driven. If I didn't have a specific reason, days could go by without me opening the LMS.
Q4: There were a few weeks where checking the LMS became more routine, especially when I was doing well in the peer support group and we'd check in with each other regularly. Having that accountability helped. But honestly, it never became truly automatic for me. The habit would form and then life would get in the way—a bad week at work or just feeling down—and I'd fall off again.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me since I learn best visually. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and examples. The discussion boards were also helpful because connecting with peers made me feel less alone in struggling with the material. When classmates explained things in their own words, sometimes it clicked better than the formal lectures. Those two things kept me going.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary readings and some of the optional practice quizzes. With limited time and low resources, I had to prioritize what would directly impact my grade. The readings felt like a luxury I couldn't afford time-wise. Some of the external links seemed useful but I just couldn't justify spending time on things that weren't required when I was already stretched thin.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or exam, or if it helped me understand something I was confused about. Visual content like videos or infographics always felt more valuable to me than walls of text. Also, if my peers were talking about something being helpful, I'd make time for it. Basically, if I could see the immediate payoff, I'd engage with it.
Q8: Yeah, optional activities felt like a nice-to-have rather than a must-do. With required activities, there's this pressure—you know it counts toward your grade. Optional stuff? I'd think 'maybe later' and later never came. It's not that I didn't see their value, but when you're juggling 12 courses and life is already heavy, you cut what you can. Required activities got done; optional ones were the first to go.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of lecture slides and PDFs early on because my internet isn't always reliable and I didn't want to depend on being online. I'd print things out when I could afford to, or study from my phone on the bus to work. I also used YouTube videos to supplement concepts that weren't clicking. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing printed notes or working through problems in a notebook, completely offline. I'd spend hours with my handwritten diagrams trying to visualize concepts. None of that showed up anywhere in the system. Or I'd be discussing course material with a friend over coffee—real learning happening, but invisible to any tracking. The LMS only saw a fraction of my effort.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF, watch or read it once on the LMS so it registered, then study it repeatedly offline. My engagement with that material was way deeper than one view would suggest. Sometimes I'd have the same diagram taped to my wall for weeks, referring back to it constantly. The initial access was just the starting point, not the whole story.
Q12: There were definitely harder periods. When I was feeling really down—and honestly, I've been dealing with some sadness lately—it was hard to muster the energy to engage. Add in stress from bills and work, and the course felt overwhelming. My attendance dropped during those times. The peer support helped pull me back, but those low points were real and affected everything.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. I'd log in, stare at the screen, and feel this mental fog. Competing responsibilities meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and working an extra shift. Fatigue made it hard to focus even when I did have time. It all compounded and showed up in my inconsistent engagement.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd have videos playing but I wasn't really absorbing anything. I'd be thinking about other problems in my life, or just too tired to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. Technically I was 'active' but mentally I was checked out. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than actual learning.
Q15: I postponed things more than I should have, especially when I was feeling low. Sometimes looking at a difficult assignment triggered this avoidance response—I knew it was important but I just couldn't face it. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would come with the same feeling. It wasn't laziness; it was more like emotional exhaustion making everything feel insurmountable.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. Without them, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all some weeks. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to log in and cram. It wasn't healthy, but it worked enough to get assignments done. My LMS activity probably spiked right before due dates and flatlined in between. That's just how I operated.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback, it boosted my mood and made me want to engage more. But critical feedback, even when constructive, sometimes made me want to avoid the course for a while. With my low resilience, negative comments hit harder than they probably should have. The peer support group helped me process feedback better than I would have alone.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me know what to prioritize. But with 12 online courses, sometimes the structures conflicted and I had to make tough choices about where to focus. When the course laid things out visually and clearly, I engaged more. When it was confusing, I'd disengage and feel frustrated.
Q19: Visual organization made engagement easier—clear timelines, video content, diagrams. What made it harder was when there was too much text-heavy content or when instructions weren't clear. Also, having too many small tasks spread out made it hard to feel like I was making progress. I needed to see the big picture to stay motivated. Confusing navigation in the LMS also wasted time I didn't have.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or stressed and they keep checking things without actually submitting. Maybe they're perfectionist and never feel ready to turn something in, or they're overwhelmed and just clicking around trying to figure out where to start. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes it's just spinning wheels while feeling stuck.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity was moderate at best, but I did okay grade-wise because a lot of my real studying happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them on my own time, away from the platform. Someone looking at my logs might think I wasn't trying hard, but the effort was there—just not visible. Logs don't capture everything.
Q22: Logs can show when you're online and what you click on, which gives a surface-level picture of engagement. But they miss the quality of that engagement—whether you're actually learning or just going through motions. They can't see offline study, emotional state, or real understanding. They definitely can't capture those moments of genuine insight that happen away from a screen. It's incomplete data.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting in real mental effort to understand it. It's not just logging in or completing tasks mechanically—it's being present and invested. For me, engagement also involves connecting with peers and feeling like I'm part of something. When I'm truly engaged, I'm thinking about the material even when I'm not studying. It's a mental and emotional investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much the peer support meant to me emotionally, or how I'd think about course concepts while doing other things. It wouldn't capture the sadness I've been carrying or how hard I've had to push through low motivation. It can't see the financial stress that limited my resources or the offline hours I spent with printed diagrams. The human side of my experience is completely invisible to the data.
